methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series B → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.9x) to $345M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 5 same-sector + same/adjacent-stage peers in Chips & Semiconductors. Range: $529M - $11.0B.

private.name: Etched
private.rationale: Same sector (Chips & Semiconductors), same stage (Series B), post-money $5B

private.name: Positron AI
private.rationale: Same sector (Chips & Semiconductors), same stage (Series B), post-money $1B+

data.narrative:
Mojo Vision, a Series B-stage semiconductor company founded by Drew Perkins and Michael Wiemer, has raised $345M to date. The valuation analysis draws on a comp universe of 11 peers—3 public (ALAB, NVDA, AMD) and 8 private companies across the chips and semiconductors sector, including later-stage players like SambaNova Systems, Etched, and d-Matrix.

Two triangulation methods are applicable. Stage-progression analysis, using total capital raised as a baseline proxy, applies a 1.9x step-up multiplier typical of Series B advancement, yielding $656M. Peer-median analysis across 5 same-sector comparables—SambaNova Systems ($11.0B, Series A), Etched ($5.0B, Series B), d-Matrix ($2.0B, Series C), Positron AI ($1.0B, Series B), and DEEPX ($529M, Series C)—produces a median valuation of $2.0B, with a range of $529M to $11.0B.

Triangulating both methods yields a valuation range of $557M (low) to $2.3B (high), with a median of $1.3B. The wide range reflects substantial heterogeneity within the peer set, particularly the outlier valuations of SambaNova and Etched. Key uncertainties include stage-specific capital efficiency at Mojo Vision relative to peers, product differentiation in a rapidly consolidating sector, and whether later-stage peers (d-Matrix, DEEPX) represent more appropriate comparables than earlier-stage, better-capitalized entrants.
